coredata.dc:title: Developing carbon dots as green modifiers for improving the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio urea-formaldehyde resin
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Adhesion and Adhesives
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Wang, Ling
Shen, An
Tang, Miao
Chen, Minzhi
Cao, Yizhong
Dai, Hongqi
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: Reducing formaldehyde-to-urea molar ratio is conducive to improving environmental-friendliness of urea-formaldehyde (UF) resin-bonded wooden products, but at the expense of bonding performance, such contradictoriness is intrinsically due to the decreased cohesion of resin polymer structures. In this study, we proposed a novel strategy to enhance the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resins by use of carbon dots (CDs) as green modifiers. Taking advantages of surface-contained plentiful carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, the synthesized CDs could generate strong physicochemical interactions with polymer segments and further improve cohesion of resin matrix. The optical microrheology demonstrated that the microscopic viscoelasticity of CDs-modified UF resin was increased as increasing of CDs content due primarily to the formation of strong hydrogen bonding interactions between CDs and resin polymers. Based on the analysis for curing behavior, the polycondensation reactivity of resin oligomers was almost free from the interaction or connection between CDs and polymer segments. Furthermore, as confirmed by nanoindentation technology, the physicochemical interactions formed between oxygenous functional groups of CDs and polymer matrices could improve the cohesion of resin after curing reaction, thus resulting in the enhanced micromechanical strength of wood-resin interphase and further bonding strength of resin at macroscopic level. Remarkably, this reinforcing effect imparted by CDs resulted in the tensile shear strength of plywood increased to 0.96 MPa, which was 77.8% increase in comparison to the panels bonded with unmodified resin and largely exceed the requirement given by national standard (0.7 MPa for plywood type Ⅱ). Taken together, this work demonstrated that the synthesized CDs are efficient and provided a potential platform for improving the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Urea-formaldehyde resin
Carbon dots
Bonding performance
Reinforcing effect
Hydrogen bonding interactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and chemical characterization for CDs
Measurement of tensile shear strength (TSS) and formaldehyde emission
Physicochemical properties of CDs-modified UF resins
Microscopic viscoelasticity of CDs-modified UF resins
Curing behavior of CDs-modified UF resins
Bonding performance of CDs-modified UF resins

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photoluminescent emission spectra of the synthesized CDs under excitation light of different wavelengths (the insert legends). (b) TEM image of the synthesized CDs, the insert image is the corresponding particle size distribution histogram. (c) FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CDs. (d) XPS survey spectrum of the synthesized CDs. The high-resolution (e) C 1s XPS and (f) O 1s XPS spectra of CDs.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs in water and UF resin excited at 360 nm, the inset photos are the samples (left to right: pure UF resin, 1#, 2#, and 3#CDs/UF resin) in room light (upper) and under a UV lamp (bottom), respectively. (b) Emission spectra of CDs in polymer matrices of UF resin under different excitation wavelengths (the inset legends). (c) TEM image of CDs-containing UF resin system.

(a) XRD pattern of pure UF resin and CDs-modified UF resins; (b) MAS 13C NMR spectra of cured resin samples of pure UF resin and 2#CDs/UF resin; (c) FTIR spectra of pure UF resin and CDs-modified UF resins; (d) FTIR spectra of pure UF resin and CDs-modified UF resins showing the variations of N–H stretching vibration.
SEM images of the (a) pure UF resin, (b) 1#CDs/UF resin, (c) 2#CDs/UF resin, and (d) 3#CDs/UF resin.
(a) Decorrelation curves of pure UF resin and (b–d) CDs-modified UF resins (the measurement time was 40 h).
Characterization for polymer viscoelasticity of CDs-modified UF resins. (a) elasticity index, (b) macroscopic viscosity index, (c) solid liquid balance, and (d) fluidity index.
(a) DSC curves and (b) conversion degree of pure resin and CDs-modified resins at a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

DEA as a monitoring system for detecting the curing process of CDs-modified UF resin in plywood fabrication. (a) Typical multi-frequent plot of log IonVisc as a function of pressing time; (b) Typical single frequency (10 Hz) plot of log IonVisc as a function of the pressing time; The curing monitoring for (c) pure UF resin and (d) 2#CDs/UF resin using 10 Hz single-frequency mode.

Mechanical strength in wood-resin interphase region obtained from NI testing. Hardness of (a) cell lumina filled with resin, and (b) wood cell walls with resin penetration. Reduced elastic modulus of (c) cell lumina filled with resin, and (d) wood cell walls with resin penetration.
(a) Comparison of formaldehyde emission and TSS of plywood bonded with pure and CDs-modified UF resins; (b) Reinforcing mechanism of the synthesized CDs in low-molar-ratio UF resin.

head.ce:title: Developing carbon dots as green modifiers for improving the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio urea-formaldehyde resin

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing formaldehyde-to-urea molar ratio is conducive to improving environmental-friendliness of urea-formaldehyde (UF) resin-bonded wooden products, but at the expense of bonding performance, such contradictoriness is intrinsically due to the decreased cohesion of resin polymer structures. In this study, we proposed a novel strategy to enhance the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resins by use of carbon dots (CDs) as green modifiers. Taking advantages of surface-contained plentiful carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, the synthesized CDs could generate strong physicochemical interactions with polymer segments and further improve cohesion of resin matrix. The optical microrheology demonstrated that the microscopic viscoelasticity of CDs-modified UF resin was increased as increasing of CDs content due primarily to the formation of strong hydrogen bonding interactions between CDs and resin polymers. Based on the analysis for curing behavior, the polycondensation reactivity of resin oligomers was almost free from the interaction or connection between CDs and polymer segments. Furthermore, as confirmed by nanoindentation technology, the physicochemical interactions formed between oxygenous functional groups of CDs and polymer matrices could improve the cohesion of resin after curing reaction, thus resulting in the enhanced micromechanical strength of wood-resin interphase and further bonding strength of resin at macroscopic level. Remarkably, this reinforcing effect imparted by CDs resulted in the tensile shear strength of plywood increased to 0.96 MPa, which was 77.8% increase in comparison to the panels bonded with unmodified resin and largely exceed the requirement given by national standard (0.7 MPa for plywood type Ⅱ). Taken together, this work demonstrated that the synthesized CDs are efficient and provided a potential platform for improving the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resins.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Urea-formaldehyde (UF) resins are the most widely used adhesives in the manufacture of wood-based panels, with this type of resin offering several superior features such as low cost, low curing temperature and short pressing time. However, the formaldehyde emission from UF resin-bonded wooden products is always a main concern to date because of its correlation with indoor air pollution and health issues [1,2]. In this context, producers have been forced to lower the formaldehyde-to-urea (F/U) molar ratio to satisfy the formaldehyde regulations worldwide, but doing so can significantly decline the bonding performance of UF resins. Such contradictoriness is determined by the conversion of resin polymer structure. Specifically, the linear polymer chains progressively become dominant as the F/U molar ratio reduced to 1.0 or below, which is adverse to forming three-dimensional network in curing reaction, and thus lead to the inferior cohesiveness in wood-resin interphase region. Thus, striking a balance between low formaldehyde emission and high bonding quality remains a significant challenge for the modification of UF resins. Melamine has been widely used by manufactures as a large-scale commercialized modifier for improving the inferior crosslink of low-molar-ratio UF resins. Through co-condensation reactions, melamine-formaldehyde resin with plenty of reactive sites can bridge with UF polymers, promoting the formation of three-dimensional polycondensates and resulting in the improvement of bonding performance. Meanwhile, the formaldehyde emission from wood composites can be lowered since the cured resin possesses excellent hydrolysis stability [3,4]. However, the effectiveness is highly dependent on the melamine dosage. In real production, the additive amount of melamine is commonly more than 20% of total amount of urea and melamine. Such large usage of melamine not only excessively consume non-renewable petrochemical derived product, but also remarkably increase the cost of wood-based panels. The intercalation of nanoclay in low-molar-ratio UF resins can also improve their cohesion through the formation of a larger branched network in their cured state [2,5], but, due to the inferior compatibility toward resin polymers, the modification effect of nanoclay closely associate with the silicate functionalization. In this regard, developing a green nanostructured modifier with hydrophilic functional groups could be more feasible to improve the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resins.

As frequently studied, the bonding strength of wood-based panels closely associate with the micromechanical properties in wood-resin interphase region, such as hardness and elastic modulus of resin penetrated cell walls [6–8]. Generally, low-molar-ratio UF resins were mainly characterized by an ordered and crystalline structure due to H-bonds between linear polymers [9]. Therefore, it is expected that incorporating nanomaterials could largely improve the original crosslinking structure. For instances, Wibowo et al. reported that transition metal ion-modified bentonite nanoclay can block hydrogen bonds between linear molecules by either intercalation or exfoliation, resulting in a less ordered polymer structure [2]. Another study revealed that cellulose nanofibrils with abundant hydroxyl groups were insufficient to intercalate the linear molecules of resins and disrupt the H-bonds between them, but the modifiers can facilitate the connection of crystalline and amorphous domains in the resins via hydrogen bonding to form the crosslinked structure [10]. Taken together, we conjecture that incorporating nanoparticles with plentiful functional groups into UF resin could improve the micromechanical strength of bond-lines by generating strong extra physicochemical interactions. In the past few years, carbon dots (CDs) have aroused a wide interest owing to their multicolor fluorescence behavior, low toxicity, photostability, and ease of surface functionalization, these advantages had made CDs promising candidates for various applications, such as anti-counterfeiting, sensing, catalysis, light-emitting diode, and photovoltaic devices [11–14]. In addition to the application of optical correlation, CDs exhibited great potential in many innovative applications due to their characteristics of nanostructure and optional functional groups, such as heightening the tribological performance of oil, alleviating the growth of dendrites in Zn anodes, moisture retention of cosmetics, and so on [15–17]. Furthermore, polymer scientists are extremely interested in exploring CDs as guest species to tailor the network structure and crosslinking behavior of polymeric materials (e.g. poly(vinyl alcohol), hydrogels and organogels). In these reported host-guest composites, the functionalized CDs can either serve as nanofillers or participate in the polymerization as monomers of initiators. Therefore, incorporating functionalized CDs into polymers is a simple, versatile, and cost-effective method that can enable the composites with desirable capabilities, including enhanced mechanical properties, anticorrosion, self-healing behavior, shape memory function, stimuli-response characteristic, and electrochemical performance [18–24]. Particularly, CDs with plenty of surface-contained oxygen- and amine-functional moieties are able to generate strong physicochemical interactions with polymer chains. The intrinsic reasons can be assigned to: (1) hydrogen bonding interactions induced by the surface functional groups of CDs (e.g. amino (−NH2), carboxyl (−COOH), and hydroxyl (−OH) groups) (2) covalent bonds formation (e.g. CO–NH), between CDs and the polymer segments, and (3) Van Der Waals bonding induced by incorporated CDs [25–27].

In this paper, we reported a new and feasible strategy to improve the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resin by using CDs as scalable and sustainable modifiers. Thereinto, thermal pyrolysis of citric acid provided an attractive platform to achieve the simple, cost-effective, and large-scale production of CDs for practical application. The use of the synthesized CDs as green modifiers could have unique effectiveness in improving the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resin. Specifically, the various oxygenous functional groups of CDs not only help in their uniform dispersion in polymer matrices upon incorporating CDs into UF resin, but also can interact with polymer segments covalently or non-covalently during the resin curing reaction, which play the vital role in improving cohesion of resin matrix. Moreover, CDs with carbonaceous cores and abundant surrounding chemical groups could restrict the chain motions by different physicochemical interactions, thus facilitating the formation of compact structural architecture in wood-resin interphase. Based on the above-mentioned reasons, the feasibility of citric acid-derived CDs in improving the bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resin was evaluated. To probe the reinforcing effect, the influence of CDs dosage on the polymer structure, viscoelasticity, curing behavior and adhesion performance of UF resin was systematically analyzed. By this way, the studies confirmed that the CDs with plentiful oxygen-containing groups (−COOH and –OH) can significantly enhance the cohesion as the resin curing completed. This structural reinforcement for low-molar ratio UF resin contributed greatly to the micromechanical strength of wood-resin interphase, and thus gave rise to the significant improvement of bonding performance.

All chemical reagents were analytical grade, and were used without further purification. Critic acid, formic acid (HCOOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Urea (99 wt%) and formalin (37 wt%) were provided by China National Medicines Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout this study.

The CDs used in present work were synthesized according to a previous report with minor modifications [28]. Typically, 100 g of citric acid in a round-bottom flask was treated in a vacuum oven at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black solid was fully dissolved in 100 mL of NaOH solution (1 mol/L) and then the pH was adjusted to 7.0 by further addition of NaOH solution. The obtained black suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered through a PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22 μm. Afterward, the collected solution containing CDs was kept for dialysis (1000 Da) with a period of 48 h. Ultimately, the solid CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying, and the purified CDs solution with a concentration of 0.41 g/mL was directly used for the modification of UF resin with low molar ratio.

The UF resin with a final F/U molar ratio of 1.0 were prepared by means of a conventional two-step alkaline-acid synthesis stage. 200 g formalin (37 wt%) was poured in a three-neck reactor, adjusted to pH 8.0–8.2 with NaOH solution, and then heated to 40 °C. Subsequently, 74 g urea was added into the reactor to obtain an initial F/U molar ratio of 2.0, and the pH was adjusted to 8.2–8.6. The addition reaction proceeded at a temperature of 90 °C. In an hour, the pH was adjusted to 4.6–4.8 with HCOOH solution for condensation reaction, and the temperature was set to 80 °C. The condensation reaction was terminated by adjusting the pH to 8.2–8.6 when the cloudy point was reached. After this reaction, another 74 g urea was added into the reactor to obtain a final F/U molar ratio of 1.0 and the temperature was maintained at 60 °C for 20 min. Ultimately, the resin prepolymer was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the pH was further adjusted to 8.2–8.6. The CDs-modified UF resin (CDs/UF resin) was prepared by the solution technique with the help of mechanical shearing. Specifically, a specific volume of CDs aqueous solution (0.41 g/mL) was added into the above synthesized UF resin and the mixture was stirred mechanically for 30 min to ensure the fully dispersion of CDs. The color changes of UF resin from milky to pale yellow in visible light and the final uniform fluorescence emission under UV light irradiation were indicators for the homogeneous dispersion of CDs. For better understanding the effectiveness of CDs-based modification as well as the dose dependent enhancement of bonding strength, the additive amount of CDs solution was increased from 100, 200–300 μL, the modified resin samples were denoted as 1#, 2#, and 3#UF/CDs resins, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and chemical characterization for CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphology of CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, Japan), the recorded TEM images were then analyzed using the software Image J to analyze the particle size distribution. The detailed compositions were determined by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyzer (XPS) (Shimadzu, UK) and Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescent spectral measurements were performed on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with excitation and emission slit set at 10 nm band pass.

The non-volatile solids content of UF resins was determined by measuring the mass before and after drying 2 g of the resin sample in an oven at 105 °C for 3 h. The viscosity of resins was determined using a cone-plate viscometer (DV-Ⅱ+, Brookfield, Middleboro, MA, USA) with a no. 2 spindle at 60 rpm, at 25 °C. The effect of CDs incorporation on the curing structure of low-molar-ratio UF resin was further investigated. Prior to the measurement, all the samples were prepared by adding 2% NH4Cl into liquid resins, and these mixtures were heated at 120 °C for 3 h in an oven. After that, the cured resin samples were ground into powder, followed by washing with deionized water to remove unreacted urea. MAS 13C magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) spectra were recorded at ambient temperature by using Bruker AVANCE Ⅲ HD 400 MHz. FT-IR detection was performed with KBr pellet technique to identify the chemical groups of pristine and CDs-modified UF resins. The crystallinity of all resin samples was characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm), the samples were scanned at room temperature from 0 to 50° with a step of 0.02°/min. The section morphology of the different cured resins was studied using a cold field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Regulus 8100, Hitachi, Japan). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed on a Q2000 calorimeter (TA, United States) to gain insight into the curing behavior of CDs-modified resins. Approximately, 5–6 mg of resin sample was mixed with NH4Cl, and then transferred to a high-pressure capsule pan to suppress the evaporation of water during DSC test. The heating temperature was increased from 30 to 160 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. The polymer viscoelasticity of the CDs-modified UF resin was investigated by an optical microrheology (Rheolaser Lab from Formulaction, French). Firstly, the liquid resin sample with volume of 20 mL was injected in a flat-bottomed cylindrical glass tube, and then the tube was transferred to sample chamber of the Rheolaser Lab preheated to 60 °C. In the process of measurement, the speckle pattern of the sample, which was generated by the interfering backscattering wave of the incident laser at 650 nm caused by the Brownian motion of the particles, was collected by a multi-pixels detector every 5 min. The generated speckle image is treated to plot the decorrelation curve for characterizing the polymer viscoelasticity of the liquid resin. Moreover, the elasticity, macroscopic viscosity index, solid-liquid balance and fluidity index of each resin sample were also measured by the optical microrheology. Dielectric analysis (DEA) was carried out for monitoring the curing process of CDs-modified UF resins. In specific, liquid resin samples containing curing agent (2% NH4Cl) were analyzed in laboratory scale between beech veneers (glue spread: 150 g/m2, dimensions: 50 × 15 × 1.2 mm, moisture content: 4%) in a laboratory hot press for isothermal measurements at the minimum pressure setting of 10 N cm−2. The interdigitated electrodes sensor was embedded in the adhesive layer and fixed between two veneers. DEA was performed in multi-frequency mode for recording the logarithmic value of the ion viscosity (log IonVisc).

The different types of plywood panels were manufactured by using eucalyptus wood veneers bonded with the prepared UF resins. Specifically, 2% of NH4Cl was added to the resin (based on the non-volatile solids content of UF resin) for promoting resin curing, and 20% of wheat flour (based on the total mass of the resin) was used as filler to adjust the viscosity of resin. Three-layer plywood panels measuring 300 × 300 × 6.2 mm (length × width × thickness) were prepared. In specific, the adhesive was applied to the both sides of center veneer at a glue spread of 250 g/m2 using a rubber roller. Three-ply veneers were cold pressed at 0.7 MPa for 20 min, and then hot-pressed at 120 °C and 1.0 MPa for 4 min. Subsequently, all plywood panels were conditioned at 20 °C for 24 h before cutting and testing.

Smaller plywood samples were prepared using the synthesized resins, and their transections were polished by ultra-microtome (Leica EM UC7, Wetzlar, Germany) for NI testing. The micro-mechanics in wood-resin interphase region (including the lumina and cell wall areas) were measured in a quasi-static loading model: Loading, holding at the peak load of 400 μN, and unloading for 5 s, respectively. The locations for NI were selected by scanning probe micrographs taken with the indenter tip. There were about 20 indents placed in valid positions, and the hardness (H) and reduced modulus of elasticity (E r) were recorded according to the Oliver and Pharr method by using the load-displacement data from the NI tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of tensile shear strength (TSS) and formaldehyde emission
ce:section.ce:para: The test for mechanical property of plywood followed the China National standard GB/T 17657-2013. The prepared plywood was cut into specimens in a size of 100 × 25 × 6.2 mm (length × width × thickness), and then two notches with a distance of 25 mm on different outer layers were cut at each specimen. Prior to the mechanical testing, all specimens were conditioned in a conditioning room (20 °C and 65% RH) for 4 weeks. After that, the specimens were merged into 63 ± 3 °C water for 3 h, and then cooled to room temperature for 10 min. The TSS measurement was performed on a mechanical testing machine (CMT6104, MTS systems (China) Co., Ltd.), and the result for each type of plywood was the mean of 56 measurements.

According to the China National standard GB/T 17657-2013, the fabricated plywood was cut into 12 specimens in a size of 150 × 50 × 6.2 mm (length × width × thickness), these samples were put in a 10 L of covered glass desiccator together with a petri dish filled with a specified amount of deionized water. The formaldehyde absorption kept for 24 h under 20 °C and then the emitted quantity of formaldehyde was determined by using a Lambda 950 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) based on the Hantzsch reaction, in which formaldehyde can react with ammonium acetate and acetylacetone, and the yield of diacetyldihydrolutidine was quantified by evaluating the characteristic UV absorption peak centered at 412 nm.

In terms of CDs synthesis strategy, the production yield and surface chemical groups as a function of the carbonaceous precursors and of the reaction conditions are essential to expand their application in polymer modification. Citric acid, as the most commonly used carbonaceous precursor, not only have advantages of low cost, renewable resource, abundance, and environmental friendliness, but also can be facilely carbonized and oxidized during pyrolysis or hydrothermal treatment, which is highly advantageous for the synthesis of CDs and equipping them with plenty of oxygenous functional groups. As an optimized example in our research, the CDs synthesized through pyrolysis of citric acid exhibited a high production yield of 42.6 wt% (based on the weight of citric acid). The fluorescence spectra of CDs presented in Fig. 1 a displays a typical excitation-dependent emission behavior, the obtained result was in accordance with previous literature [28], indicating the successful synthesis of CDs in our research. As shown in Fig. 1b, TEM image revealed that the synthesized CDs were uniform spherical particles dispersing in water, and the particle size ranged from 3 to 8 nm with an average diameter of 5.4 nm. It should be mentioned that the CDs morphology observed from TEM image only reflect the carbonaceous structures, while, in addition to sp2 carbon skeleton, the typical CDs are also composed of abundant functional groups or polymer chains [29], which is completely different from the inorganic nanoparticles. The surface functional groups of CDs were identified by FTIR, as shown in Fig. 1c. The stretching vibration of –OH, C–H and C–O groups exhibited absorption peaks at 3424, 2927 and 1210 cm−1, respectively [30]. The emerging peak at 1633 cm−1 suggested the presence of conjugated aromatic structure [31], while the neighboring peak at 1710 cm−1 with high absorption intensity was assigned to the carboxylic CO bonds on CDs surfaces [32–34], demonstrating that carboxyl groups on the surface were continuously enriched to form carboxyl CDs during the carbonization of citric acid molecules. Moreover, the merged peaks at 1386 and 1575 cm−1 were stemmed from the characteristic absorption of symmetric and antisymmetric stretches of carboxylate ions [28]. More insights into the surface functional groups of CDs can be obtained from XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1d, the full-survey spectrum displayed three peaks at 285, 497, and 532 eV, attributed to C (71.5%), Na (3.4%), and O (25.1%) elements, it should be mentioned that the small amount of Na element was from solvent residue (NaOH solution), this result was also confirmed by FTIR findings. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s shown in Fig. 1e consisted of three types of C: CC/C–C (284.6 eV), C–OH (286.1 eV), and –COOH (288.8 eV) [30,35]. Furthermore, two peaks located at 531.7 and 533.3 eV can be observed in the O 1s spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 1f, which can be attributed to the carboxylic CO and C–OH bonds [30]. According to the above analysis, the hydrophilic surfaces can contribute greatly to CDs dispersion in polymer matrices of UF resin without aggregation and macroscopic phase separation. Importantly, the intrinsic high specific surface area and the existence of abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were conducive to forming highly dense hydrogen bonds with polymer segments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of CDs-modified UF resins
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersity of CDs in polymer matrices can be evaluated by fluorescence emission under UV light. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs in polymer matrices showed decreased fluorescence intensity compared to the aqueous solution due to the light shielding effect of polymer chains of UF resin. Despite this, the bright and uniform fluorescence can be clearly observed under UV light of 365 nm (the inset in Fig. 2a), indicating the even distribution of CDs in resin matrix. Furthermore, the excitation-dependent emission behavior of hybrid resin systems was nearly the same as that of CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 2b), because the matrices can act as media like water, where CDs did not suffer self-quenching induced by aggregation [19]. The inner structure of CDs-containing resin system (2#CDs/UF resin) was probed by TEM technique. As seen in Fig. 2c, the CDs inside polymer matrices displayed uniform dispersion, which was attributed to the excellent compatibility between hydrophilic CDs and resin molecules.

The solid content of all resin samples was determined to be about 54.5%, and the viscosity was about 210 mPa s at room temperature. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the XRD patterns demonstrated that the crystallinity of low-molar-ratio UF resin was decreased with the increase of CDs dosage, but the difference was not significant. According to previous researches, since the second addition of urea at the final synthesis stage disrupt oxymethylene and branched methylene ether linkages, the resulting linear polymer chains can readily form crystalline domains by intramolecular hydrogen bonding interactions [36]. In this case, the participation of CDs in the curing reaction of UF resin is difficult to decrease the resin crystallinity regardless of additive amount, because, unlike nanoclay with two-dimensional structure, the nano-sized CDs were scarcely possible to disrupt the hydrogen bonding interactions between resin polymer chains by intercalation effect. Shown in Fig. 3b is the MAS 13C NMR spectra of cured resin samples of pure UF resin and 2#CDs/UF resin, as seen, there are four peaks in 13C NMR signals of the two resins, including –NH–CO–NH− at 158.81 ppm, –NH–CH2OH at 64.41 ppm, and Ⅱ-type methylene at 54.37 ppm, and Ⅰ-type methylene at 46.28 ppm [37]. The obtained result elucidated that the cured UF resin was mainly composed of amide groups as the main body and the network structure bridged by methylene groups. Taken together, it is evident that the incorporation of CDs can hardly affect the chemical moieties of cured UF resin. The possible interactions between CDs and resin polymers were also probed FTIR analysis, as shown in Fig. 3c. For the pure resin, the broad absorption band at 3345 cm−1 was mainly assigned to the stretching vibration of N–H moieties in the linear resin polymers. The stretching vibration of C–H groups displayed absorption peak at 2960 cm−1. The peak appearing at around 1637 cm−1 were related to the CO of primary amides. The secondary and tertiary amide showed absorption peaks at 1552 and 1238 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 1384, 1134, and 1031 cm−1 were indicative of C–N in amide, C–O–C in dimethylene ether linkage, and C–O in hydroxymethyl, respectively [10]. As for the CDs-modified UF resins, no additional absorption peaks can be detected in FTIR spectra, which should be ascribed to the low additive amount of CDs and their similar oxygen-related groups with resin polymers. Nonetheless, the IR absorption peak of amide group (with wavenumbers of 1552 and 1238 cm−1) showed decreasing tendency as increasing dosage of CDs, it is reasonable to deduce that amidation reaction between carboxyl on CDs surfaces and amino groups in resin oligomers could compete against polycondensation of oligomers in curing reaction. Furthermore, it can be found that the peak position of N–H groups distinctly shifted from 3345 cm−1–3367 cm−1 (Fig. 3d). Generally, hydrogen bonds are formed based on the reason that the oxygen atoms of carboxyl or hydroxyl groups withdraw electron from the hydrogen atoms of –NH− and –NH2−, which commonly lead to the redshift of stretching vibration peak of amino groups [2,38]. Taken together, the nanostructured CDs, with plentiful hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, allowed polymer chains to penetrate the CDs surface, maximizing the contact area between these nanoparticles and polymer segments along with the formation of strong physicochemical bonding forces.

Based on the SEM images in Fig. 4 a, the section morphology of pure UF resin clearly exhibited spherical structure originated from the growth of colloid particles, which is a typical characteristic of cured UF resin with low molar ratio. In contrast, the dense microstructure formed by colloid particles was more distinct with the increase of CDs content, as illustrated in Fig. 4b–d. Therefore, the incorporation of CDs could lead to heterogeneous nucleation of micelle during the resin curing process. The underlying reason was that the plentiful hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CDs surfaces were of great importance for generating strong physicochemical interactions with resin polymers, which promoted the efficient bridge or even strong entanglement of resin polymer segments. According to some classical reinforcing/toughening mechanisms, like segment level tight nanoparticle bridging [39], we believe that the hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding between the CDs and resin polymer chains were in favor to form strong and large networks within the cured resin structures, which could lead to aggregated growth of colloid particles, and eventually improved the strength and toughness of the bond-lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic viscoelasticity of CDs-modified UF resins
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous report, the micro-rheological behavior and polymer viscoelasticity of the CDs-modified UF resins can be well evaluated by using optical microrheology [40]. As shown in Fig. 5 , the mean square displacement (MSD) of resin polymer as a function of decorrelation time was recorded. Typically, the MSD curves can be divided three regions, the linear curves at initial region indicate the viscosity of polymer matrices, in which case the lower slope commonly means lower viscosity, and vice versa. The second part of the MSD curves with the presence of platform region indicates the macroscopic elasticity of polymer matrices. In general, the low platform indicates the formation of a tight network structure with relatively high elasticity, while the high platform suggests that the network structure constructed by the polymer segments is relatively loose and the elasticity is relatively low. The rightmost region of MSD curves is closely related to the viscosity of polymer matrices, and the slope of curve is indicative of the viscosity. Based on above principles, the MSD curves provide a strong support that the viscosity of pure UF resin, 1# CDs/UF resin, and 2#CDs/UF resin was increased when the test duration extended to 40 h, while the elasticity of these resin samples didn't show any notable changes because there was almost no platform region presented in the MSD curves. For the pure UF resin and 1#CDs/UF resin, the decorrelation time for MSD curves was below 10−1 s, indicating that the two resin samples retained their flow characteristic in the measurement period, while for the 2#CDs/UF resin, the decorrelation time for MSD curves was greater than 103 s when the testing spent more than 8 h, illustrating that the viscosity of 2#CDs/UF was prone to increase during its storage period. Similarly, along with the extension of time, the viscosity of 3#CDs/UF resin was also increased. In sharp contrast, the MSD curves recorded from 3#CDs/UF resin were inversed and showed distinct platform as the measurement time increased above 14 h, Thus, it is reasonable to deduce that increasing the dosage of CDs would lead to a decrease in stability. We conjectured that, with the water evaporation in the test period of MSD, CDs with plenty of oxygenous groups possessed an inherent tendency to interact with polymer segments of UF resin, the higher dosage of CDs was added into the polymer matrix, the stronger the interaction was generated as water separating out. This resulted in restrictions in the macromolecular chain motion and thus gave rise to the increased viscosity of the resin as the storage time prolonged. As such, when the viscosity began to increase, the modified resin sample became unstable at macroscopic level.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the time-dependent elasticity of each resin sample was comparatively analyzed, and the results demonstrated that the elasticity of 3#CDs/UF resin increased significantly with the extension of measurement time, followed by the others in the order 2#CDs/UF > 1#CDs/UF > pure UF resin. The macroscopic viscosity of 1#CDs/UF resin was the same as that of pure UF resin. For the 2#CDs/UF resin, the viscosity had a slight increasing tendency when the measurement time was extended to 20 h, and such performance increased sharply as the testing period exceeded 20 h. In comparison, the as-prepared 3#CDs/UF resin demonstrated the highest viscosity at initial period, and also exhibited sharp increase within a few hours (Fig. 6b). The solid-liquid balance (with a balance value of 0.5) and fluidity index of resins modified with CDs were illustrated in Fig. 6c and d. In comparison to the pure resin and 1#CDs/UF resin, the 2#CDs/UF resin gradually lost its fluidity as time went on, while the transformation of 3#CDs/UF resins from liquid to solid was more obvious. The intrinsic reason should be attributed to the enhanced physicochemical interactions within the hybrid resin system. Based on the above evidence, CDs with carbonaceous cores and large peripheral polar functional groups could largely restrict the macromolecular chain motion by forming H-bonds with polymer segments or inducing entanglement of polymer chains. Attributed to the strong interactions, more compact polymer architecture could be formed during the curing process of hybrid UF resins as increasing CDs usage. But from another view, increasing CDs incorporation could also decline the storage stability of UF resin. Considering that the storage stability of UF resins is also an important factor for their practical application, the 2#CDs/UF resin was taken as an example for detailed analysis in the following discussion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curing behavior of CDs-modified UF resins
ce:section.ce:para: The curing behavior of CDs-modified UF resin was investigated by DSC analysis. as shown in Fig. 7 a, the exothermic profile is commonly attributed to the polycondensation reaction between amino groups and hydroxymethyl groups of UF resin oligomers [2,41]. As seen, the DSC exothermic peak temperature determined from pure resin and CDs-modified resins displayed negligible difference, indicating that the reactivity of resin oligomers was almost free from the interaction or connection between CDs and polymer segments. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the negligible difference in temperature-dependent conversion degree of resin samples also witnessed the above argument.

DEA measurement provide an efficient platform to gain insight into the actual curing process of CDs-modified resins, because dielectric conductivity relying on crosslinking reaction can be real-time measured during the formation of bond-lines. According to previous researches, the variation of dielectric property can be described using the conductivity term, and furthermore, the ion viscosity term, which is a multiplicative inverse of the ion conductivity, reflects crosslinking and drying process even better [42,43]. The changes of curing properties for the low-molar-ratio UF resin with and without CDs modification were studies in hot-pressing process. Fig. 8 a displays the typical multi-frequent DEA-signal collected from the adhesive bond-lines that was formed at the condition of hot-pressing. Remarkably, the ion viscosity was decreased at initial stage, which associated with the increase of temperature and proton formation (induced by acid catalysis of NH4Cl). As the temperature was high enough to initiate the crosslinking reaction, the ion viscosity started to increase, and then the further increase was mainly related to both curing reaction and water evaporation. Moreover, it can be found that the DEA signals were dependent on the applied frequency, because the migration rate of ions and dipoles were also affected by the alternating electrical field. Due to the relaxation loss of dipole, the multi-frequent curves showed distinct bifurcation as the frequency increased above 20 Hz, while the signals obtained at lower frequencies (5, 10, and 20 Hz) were almost consistent with each other. Based on the testing principle of DEA technique, the ions migration could be better reflected by DEA signals at lower frequencies. In this regard, the cure kinetics and viscosity variations of CDs-modified resins were further studied by 10 Hz single-frequency mode, as shown in Fig. 8b. Although the curing kinetics of UF resins were detected at a press temperature of 120 °C, the adhesive bond-lines were formed under non-isothermal condition due to the heat transferring and absorbing process of wood veneers. It can be seen that the temperature in bond-lines increased almost linearly over the 70 s of the pressing time, such a rapid increase of temperature was likely due to the efficient heat transmission from the thin veneers. Then, a brief plateau at a temperature slightly above 100 °C can be observed because of the consumption of energy for water evaporation from the resin and wood veneers. After that, the temperature was gradually increased to the set temperature. For the DEA signals obtained from the four resin samples, the curves of log IonVisc showed similar pattern, the signals at initial stage that was related to crosslinking reaction of resin were slightly different. In specific, as shown in Fig. 8c, the crosslink of pure UF resin occurred under production conditions with a pressing time of 3.2 min at 105 °C. For the 2#CDs/UF resin (Fig. 8d), the onset of crosslinking reaction was detected as the temperature increased to 110 °C and the pressing time prolonged to 3.8 min. As it was confirmed by above mentioned analysis, the curing behavior of low-molar-ratio UF resin was nearly unaffected by CDs incorporation.

To evaluate the adhesion strength of CDs-modified UF resins, the hardness (H) and reduced elastic modulus (E r) of cell lumina filled with resin as well as cell walls with resin penetration were analyzed using NI technology. Compared with the control, CDs incorporation imparted a positive effect on the H and E r of wood-resin interphase. Besides that, an obvious trend for the modified resins is that the larger usage of CDs resulted in the higher cohesion of bond-lines. Taking the sample bonded with 2#CDs/UF resin as an example, the average H values determined from wood-resin interphase region were 0.94 GPa for cell lumina and 0.86 GPa for cell walls, which was 34.37% and 36.51% increase compared with those determined from sample bonded with pure UF resin (as seen in Fig. 9 a and b). Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 9c and d, the average E r of cell lumina in the sample bonded with 2#CDs/UF resin was 10.58 GPa, which is 21.05% increase compared with the control. In terms of E r obtained from cell walls in wood-resin interphase, the 2#CDs/UF resin also outperformed pure resin. Specifically, the mean E r value of samples bonded with pure resin was 12.50 GPa, while the cell walls with 2#CDs/UF resin penetration showed average E r value of 14.91 GPa. The aforementioned results demonstrated that the strong physicochemical interactions between CDs and resin polymers gave rise to reinforcing effect on wood-resin interphase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bonding performance of CDs-modified UF resins
ce:section.ce:para: As revealed in Fig. 10 a, the formaldehyde emission of plywood samples was in the range of 0.21–0.23 mg/L, revealing that the modification of low-molar-ratio UF resin with CDs had negligible effect on the formaldehyde emission of plywood panels. This is because the synthesized CDs had few reactive sites towards free formaldehyde in polymer matrices. For each type of plywood, the bonding strength of 56 specimens were measured and all the specimens failed within the notches and at the bonding interphase. The wood-resin interphase with the fortification of CDs endowed the plywood with enhanced bonding strength (Fig. 10a). Specifically, the TSS of plywood bonded with pure UF resin was 0.54 MPa, which cannot meet the minimum requirement specified in National Standard of People's Republic of China GB/T 9846-2015: plywood type Ⅱ (TSS value of 0.7 MPa). In contrast, the low-molar-ratio UF resin modified with CDs yielded enhanced bonding strength results. For the plywood panels fabricated with 1#CDs/UF resin, the TSS can be increased to 0.82 MPa. Furthermore, the 2# and 3#CDs/UF resins showed relative higher bonding performance, endowing the plywood panels with average TTS of 0.93 and 0.98 MPa, respectively. These results largely exceeded the requirement given by national standard. For better understanding the mechanism of reinforcing behavior, a sketch diagram of physicochemical interactions between CDs and UF resin polymers was drawn according to the above discussion, as seen in Fig. 10b. As already discussed, the synthesized CDs with plenty of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups can serve as crosslinking sites with a result of generating dense covalent and non-covalent bonds. Thus, as increasing CDs dosage, the cohesion of the resin enhanced by the formation of more entangled networks and crosslinked structures in resin matrix. This structural reinforcement endowed wood-resin interphase with high strength and toughness, and further contributed greatly to improving bonding strength of plywood at macroscopic level.

In summary, we present a novel, simple, and cost-effective strategy to improve bonding performance of low-molar-ratio UF resins by incorporating CDs as green modifiers. Although the participation of CDs in curing reaction can hardly alter the crystalline structure and chemical moieties of resin matrix, the CDs with surface-contained abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were prone to generate dense H-bonds with polymer matrices. As such, the polymer viscoelasticity of hybrid resin system was progressively increased with increasing the dosage of CDs. The curing behavior of CDs-modified UF resin was investigated by DEA and DSC analysis, the comparative results illustrated that incorporating CDs did not block the polycondensation of UF resin oligomers, thus showing negligible effect on the curing reaction of UF resin. As revealed by the test for plywood bonded with modified resins, the CDs-based modification was insufficient to lower the formaldehyde emission, presumably owing to the lack of reactive sites of CDs towards formaldehyde. Despite that, these unique nanostructures containing carbonaceous cores and abundant surrounding chemical groups can covalently or non-covalently interact with polymer matrices, which could enhance the crosslinking degree of resin matrix by facilitating the formation of entangled networks and more crosslinked structures in curing reaction of UF resin. As such, the micro mechanical strengths of wood-resin interphase, including hardness and reduced elastic modulus, were enhanced significantly. In macroscopic view, the reinforcing effect imparted by CDs contributed greatly to improving the bonding strength of plywood, resulting in that the TSS of plywood dramatically increased from 0.54 to 0.96 MPa.

Pei Yang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review & Editing, Funding acquisition. Ziqi Zhu: Investigation. Ling Wang: Investigation. An Shen: Investigation. Miao Tang: Investigation. Minzhi Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yizhong Cao: Investigation. Hongqi Dai: Conceptualization. Xiaoyan Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32101460, 32271784), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2020M681624), the Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province (2021K042A), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFD2200602-1), and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unexpected role of amphiphilic lignosulfonate to improve the storage stability of urea formaldehyde resin and its application as adhesives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao S., Cheng Z., Zhou X., Liu Y., Chen R., Wang J., et al., Unexpected role of amphiphilic lignosulfonate to improve the storage stability of urea formaldehyde resin and its application as adhesives, Int. J. Biol. Macromol. 161 (2020) 755-762.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dielectric analysis as a cure monitoring system for UF particle boards

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Muller U., Pretschuh C., Mitter R., Knappe S., Dielectric analysis as a cure monitoring system for UF particle boards, Int. J. Adhes. Adhes. 73 (2017) 45-50.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of dielectric analysis for monitoring the cure process of phenol formaldehyde adhesive

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sernek M., Kamke F.A., Application of dielectric analysis for monitoring the cure process of phenol formaldehyde adhesive, Int. J. Adhes. Adhes. 27 (2007) 562-567.
